title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Your Own Intuition
meta_description: Stop second guessing your intuition by using it to evaluate itself. Learn why this loop happens and how to break free. Practical clarity for sensitive people.
og_title: How to Use Intuition When Evaluating Your Own Intuition
og_description: Stop second guessing your intuition by using it to evaluate itself. Learn why this loop happens and how to break free. Practical clarity for sensitive people.
body:
The Trap of Second-Guessing Your Own Knowing

You've been there. You get a hit of intuition - a strong knowing that something is true, or a clear nudge to take a certain action. And then, almost immediately, your brain jumps in. "Is that really intuition? Or is it just wishful thinking? Anxiety? A memory from something similar that happened before? How do you even know the difference?"

And suddenly, you're not just having an intuitive experience. You're evaluating it. And you're using your intuition to evaluate your intuition. Which feels like trying to lift yourself off the ground by your own shoelaces. Right?!

I'm Kalesh, and I've seen this loop wreck more sensitive people than almost anything else. You're not broken. You're not making this up. But the way you're trying to check your intuition is actually what's making you doubt it. Let me show you what I mean.

Why Intuition Folds Under Direct Scrutiny

Here's the thing most people don't tell you: intuition is not built to be analyzed. It's built to be received. When you turn the full beam of your conscious mind onto an intuitive impression, you're essentially shining a spotlight on a candle flame. The flame doesn't get brighter - it gets harder to see.

Think about it. When you're trying to remember someone's name, what happens when you really bear down and strain to pull it up? It stays gone. But the moment you relax, stop trying, and go do something else - boom, it's right there. Intuition works the same way. The more you grab at it, the more it slips through your fingers.

Does that land? Because this is the core problem with trying to evaluate your own intuition using your own intuition. You're asking the same system to both generate the data and audit the data at the same time. That's not a reliable process. It's like asking a fish to evaluate whether it's swimming correctly - it just is, until you make it self-conscious.

The Self-Referencing Loop That Makes You Crazy

Let me paint you a picture of how this actually plays out. You get a feeling about a decision - maybe it's about a relationship, a job move, or whether to say something to someone. The feeling is clear. It's a yes, or a no, or a "wait." You feel it in your body. It's calm, certain, unforced.

Then the voice comes in: "But what if that's just fear? What if you're projecting? What if you want it to be true so badly that you're making it up?" And because you're a sensitive, conscientious person, you take that question seriously. You don't want to be deluded. You want to be accurate.

So you try to check. You sit down and try to feel again - this time with the added pressure of "I need to get this right." And what do you feel? Nothing. Or confusion. Or a mix of signals that could mean anything. So you try again. And again. And each time, you trust yourself a little less.

The problem isn't that your intuition is unreliable. The problem is that you're asking it to do two jobs at once - and the second job (self-evaluation) is actively sabotaging the first (receiving).

This is the bright wound. You're so committed to being accurate that you've learned to distrust the very instrument you're trying to use. And that's not your fault. You were taught that feelings are suspect, that you need proof, that you need to cross-check everything. But intuition doesn't work that way.

How to Tell Real Intuition from Noise - Without Breaking the Spell

So what do you actually do? You can't just blindly follow every impulse that shows up. Some of them are fear. Some are old patterns. Some are actually just indigestion. You need some way to sort the signal from the noise. But the sorting process can't destroy the signal in the process.

Here's the key insight: you don't evaluate intuition with more intuition. You evaluate it with context, with time, and with a different kind of attention. Let me break that down.

1. Use Your Body, Not Your Brain

The first filter is physical. Real intuition has a specific somatic signature. It's usually calm, neutral, and clear. It doesn't feel urgent, panicked, or desperate. It doesn't shout. It just is. When you get a hit, don't ask "Is this true?" Ask instead: "Where do I feel this in my body, and what's the quality of that sensation?"

If it's tight, hot, racing, or gripping - that's likely fear or anxiety. If it's expansive, still, cool, or a simple knowing - that's more likely intuition. This isn't foolproof, but it's a much better starting point than trying to think your way through it.

You want to get better at this? Start tracking it. Write down what you felt, where you felt it, and what happened later. Over time, you'll build a personal map of your own intuitive body language. That's way more useful than any external system.

2. Check for the "No Effort" Test

Real intuition doesn't require effort. It arrives. It's just there. You don't have to strain, concentrate, or "try to feel." If you're working hard to get a read on something, you're probably in your head, constructing a feeling rather than receiving one.

This is huge. If you're sitting there going "Okay, what's my intuition saying? Let me really focus... let me clear my mind... let me try to sense..." - stop. You've already left the station. Intuition is passive reception, not active extraction. If it's not coming easily, it's not coming. Move on and come back later.

I know that sounds counterintuitive. But think about it: when you really know something, you don't have to convince yourself. You just know. The same is true for intuition. If you're having to argue yourself into believing it, you're probably not dealing with intuition.

3. Use a Third-Party Reference Point

This is where the self-referencing loop breaks open. Instead of trying to evaluate your intuition from inside your own head, bring in something external. Not to replace your knowing, but to give it a mirror.

One of the best tools I've found for this is a good book on intuitive development that gives you a approach for understanding what you're experiencing. It's not about following someone else's rules - it's about having a map so you can recognize the terrain you're walking through. I recommend checking out Sonia Choquette's "The Psychic's Handbook" on Amazon (paid link). She lays out the different channels of intuition in a way that helps you name what you're experiencing, which alone can cut the confusion in half.

Another approach is to use a tool like tarot or oracle cards. Not because the cards have magic powers, but because they give you a structured way to check your read. You look at the card, you note your first impression, and then you compare it to the traditional meaning. If they match, you're probably on track. If they don't, you might be projecting. It's a simple reality check that doesn't require you to doubt yourself.

The Real Problem Is Trust, Not Technique

Let's be honest. Most of the time, you already know. You know what your intuition is saying. You just don't trust it. You're waiting for a sign that's more certain, more undeniable, more impossible to misinterpret. But that sign rarely comes. And while you're waiting, you're second-guessing the quiet voice that already spoke.

I've worked with hundreds of sensitive people, and the pattern is always the same. They come to me saying "I can't tell what's intuition and what's fear." And within ten minutes of conversation, it's obvious they know. They just don't want to admit it, because admitting it means they have to act on it. And acting on it means risk. And risk means they might be wrong.

So the real question isn't "How do I evaluate my intuition?" It's "How do I learn to trust myself enough to act on what I already know?"

4. The Timeline Trick

Here's a practical method that works. When you get a hit of intuition, don't try to evaluate it in the moment. Instead, write it down. Date it. Describe the feeling and what it's pointing to. Then put it away and don't look at it for at least a week. Longer if possible.

When you come back to it, read what you wrote. How does it feel now? Does it still ring true? Has something happened that confirms or contradicts it? The distance gives you perspective without requiring you to dissect the experience while it's happening. You're not evaluating your intuition in real time - you're reviewing the record later. That's a completely different process, and it's way more reliable.

This is also how you build trust over time. Every time you write down a hit and it turns out to be accurate, you're laying down a brick of evidence for yourself. After a few dozen bricks, you stop needing to check every single one. You just know your system works.

5. Stop Trying to Be Objective

This one's going to sting a little. You've been taught that objectivity is the gold standard. That you should set aside your feelings, your biases, your personal investment, and look at things "as they really are." But that's not how intuition works. Intuition is inherently subjective. It's your system, reading your environment, through your filters. There's no such thing as pure, unfiltered intuition.

So stop trying to achieve it. Instead, learn to work with your filters. Know what your common distortions are. Are you prone to wishful thinking? Do you tend to assume the worst? Do you have a pattern of trusting people too quickly, or not quickly enough? These aren't bugs. They're just data. When you know your biases, you can account for them without having to pretend they don't exist.

This is where a more complete resource can really help. Check out "The Gift of Fear" by Gavin de Becker on Amazon (paid link). It's not a psychic book, but it's one of the best resources ever written on how to trust your gut in high-stakes situations. De Becker shows you how to distinguish between real intuitive warnings and the noise of overthinking. It's a masterclass in practical discernment.

What to Do When You're Stuck in the Loop

Okay, so you're in the middle of it. You're trying to figure out if a feeling is real or not, and you can feel yourself spinning. What do you do in that moment?

First, stop. Literally stop what you're doing. Stand up, walk away from whatever you're trying to evaluate. Go drink water. Look out a window. Pet your dog. Do anything that breaks the mental grip of the loop.

Second, externalize. Talk to someone you trust. Not to get their opinion, but to hear yourself say it out loud. There's something about verbalizing an intuitive hit that clarifies it. You'll hear in your own voice whether it rings true or not.

Third, set a timer. Give yourself permission to not know for a specific amount of time. Say "I'm not going to decide about this for 48 hours." Then let it go. When you come back to it, the answer will usually be obvious. The urgency was the only thing making it confusing.

6. Use a Simple Decision Tree

Here's a quick approach you can use when you're genuinely stuck. It's not perfect, but it's better than spinning:

  • Does this feeling come with a sense of calm certainty, or does it feel urgent and anxious? (Calm = more likely intuition)
  • Does this feeling point toward action or avoidance? (Intuition often points toward action, even if it's scary)
  • If I follow this feeling, what's the worst that could happen? Can I handle that?
  • If I ignore this feeling, will it keep coming back? (Persistent quiet nudges are usually real)
  • Does this feeling align with my values and long-term wellbeing, or does it feel reactive?

Run through those questions, and you'll usually get a clear answer. And if you don't? Then the answer is probably "not yet." Real intuition is patient. It doesn't demand immediate action. It just waits.

The Deeper Work: Healing the Relationship with Yourself

At this point, I need to say something that might be uncomfortable. The reason you struggle to trust your intuition isn't because your intuition is broken. It's because you've been trained not to trust yourself. Somewhere along the way, you learned that your perceptions are unreliable. That your feelings are too much. That you need an outside authority to validate what you already know.

That training is the bright wound. It's the scar from being told, over and over, that you're wrong about what you feel. And that scar makes you reach for external validation even when you don't need it.

Healing that wound isn't about finding a better technique. It's about rebuilding trust with yourself, one small act of faith at a time. Every time you follow a quiet intuitive nudge and it works out, you're healing. Every time you write down a hit and come back to find it was accurate, you're healing. Every time you choose to trust yourself over the voice of doubt, even for a moment, you're healing.

This is why I recommend keeping a dedicated intuition journal. Not a fancy one - just a notebook where you track your hits, your misses, and your body's signals. Over time, it becomes your personal reference manual. You can look back and see the patterns. You can see that you're actually way more accurate than you give yourself credit for.

And if you want a structured guide for that process, "The Intuitive Way" by Penney Peirce on Amazon (paid link) is a fantastic companion. It walks you through the stages of developing trust in your own perception, with exercises that actually work. It's not about becoming psychic - it's about becoming reliable to yourself.

The Final Paradox: You Have to Stop Evaluating to Know

Here's where we end up, and it's a paradox you're going to have to sit with. The more you try to evaluate your intuition, the less access you have to it. The more you relax, trust, and just receive, the clearer it becomes. The evaluation happens after the fact, not during.

This doesn't mean you should blindly follow every impulse. It means you need a different relationship with the process. You receive first, without judgment. You write it down. You check it against your body. You give it time. And then, later, when you're not in the middle of it, you evaluate. But in the moment? You just receive.

That's the skill. That's the practice. And it's hard, because your brain wants to be in control. But control is the enemy of intuition. Surrender is the doorway. Not blind surrender - surrendered awareness. You stay awake, you stay present, and you let the knowing come without grabbing at it.

You can do this. You've been doing it your whole life, just without trusting it. Now it's time to trust. Start small. Start today. Pick one small thing you already know, and act on it. See what happens. Then do it again. That's how you build the muscle. That's how you heal the bright wound.

And if you want more help with this process, I've put together a free assessment that helps you identify exactly where your intuitive trust is blocked. It takes about ten minutes, and it'll show you the specific pattern that's keeping you stuck. You can find it at the link below.

Take the Intuitive Trust Assessment here - it'll show you exactly what's in the way of trusting your own knowing.
